turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court in case 23-1275, Medina versus Planned Parenthood, South Atlantic.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: The federal Medicaid statute offers states a bargain. When states accept federal Medicaid funds, they must agree to spend the money in accordance with various statutory conditions. One of those conditions, the so-called any qualified provider provision, requires states to ensure that any individual eligible for medical assistance may obtain it from any provider qualified to perform the service. Everyone agrees that if a state fails to comply with that condition, or many other conditions listed in the Medicaid statute, the federal government may withhold some or all of the state's Medicaid funding. This case poses the question whether in addition to that remedy, individual Medicaid recipients may sue state officials for failing to comply with the statute's any qualified provider provision. The Medicaid statute itself does not give individuals permission to sue. Recognizing as much, the plaintiff in this case, Julie Edwards, asserts that a separate federal statute, 42 U.S.C. Section 1983, permits suits to enforce the any qualified provider provision. Section 1983, Ms. Edwards observes, authorizes private parties to sue state actors who violate their rights under the Constitution and laws of the United States. And she says, the any qualified provider provision confers on Medicaid recipients an individual right, enforceable under 1983, to select preferred health care providers. We disagree. Federal statutes do not confer rights for purposes of 1983 as a matter of course under this Court's precedents. And that's particularly true of statutes like Medicaid that are enacted pursuant to Congress' spending power. The spending power permits Congress to offer funds to states with conditions attached. In that sense, spending power legislation resembles a contract. States agree to give up something in exchange for federal funds. When a state fails to comply with conditions in spending power legislation, the typical remedy, again, is for the federal government to cut off the state's funding. If Congress wishes to depart from that norm by authorizing private enforcement suits our precedents have long held, it must speak clearly and unambiguously. That way, a state knows that it must answer such suits as a condition of the federal government's offer, allowing it to voluntarily choose whether or not to accept federal funds on those terms. For those reasons, we have recognized that spending power legislation cannot provide the basis for a Section 1983 enforcement suit unless Congress speaks with a clear voice, and manifests an unambiguous intent to confer an individual right, nothing less than that will suffice. Those long-standing principles, which this Court reaffirmed just two terms ago in a case called Talevsky, resolve today's dispute. In contrast to other statutes that confer enforceable rights under Section 1983, the any qualified provider provision nowhere mentions rights, nor does it focus on an individual's entitlement. Instead, the provision speaks to what a state must do to participate in Medicaid. Doubtless, like most legislation, perhaps all, the provision seeks to benefit people, but the statute does not clearly and unambiguously deploy rights creating language. The surrounding statutory context confirms as much. For one, the any qualified provider provision appears in paragraph 23 of 87 within a subsection titled Contents that outlines scores of things a state's Medicaid plan must include to qualify for federal funding. All of those requirements are directed not to the rights of individuals, but to the Secretary of Health and Human Services, who must approve any plan that meets them. For another, to continue receiving federal funding, the Medicaid statute says, a state need only comply substantially with the any qualified provider mandate. That focus on aggregate compliance suggests the statute addresses the state's obligations to the federal government, not the rights of any particular person. Observe as well what it would mean if the any qualified provider provision did create an individually enforceable rights. Many other Medicaid plan requirements would likely do the same. And instead of remaining atypical exceptions, as our decision, and Talevsky said they are, rights creating provisions might become more nearly the rule. For these reasons and others detailed in our opinion, the judgment of the Fourth Circuit is reversed, and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan have joined.
